The **CAF Confederation Cup**, known as the **[TotalEnergies](/wiki/TotalEnergies "TotalEnergies") CAF Confederation Cup** (*Coupe de la confédération de la CAF TotalEnergies* in [French](/wiki/French_language "French language")) for sponsorship purposes, is an annual [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") [club](/wiki/Football_team "Football team") competition established in 2004 and organized by [CAF](/wiki/Confederation_of_African_Football "Confederation of African Football").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Clubs qualify for the competition based on their performance in their national leagues and cup competitions. It is the second-tier competition of African club football, ranking below the [CAF Champions League](/wiki/CAF_Champions_League "CAF Champions League"). The winner of the tournament faces the winner of the aforementioned competition in the following season's [CAF Super Cup](/wiki/CAF_Super_Cup "CAF Super Cup").

[Moroccan](/wiki/Royal_Moroccan_Football_Federation "Royal Moroccan Football Federation") clubs have the highest number of victories (eight titles), followed by Tunisia with five. Morocco has the largest number of winning teams, with five clubs having won the title. The competition has been won by 14 clubs, six of which have won it more than once. [RS Berkane](/wiki/RS_Berkane "RS Berkane") and [CS Sfaxien](/wiki/CS_Sfaxien "CS Sfaxien") are the most successful clubs in the competition's history, having won the tournament three times each. [RS Berkane](/wiki/RS_Berkane "RS Berkane") are the current defending champions, having beaten [Simba S.C.](/wiki/Simba_S.C. "Simba S.C.") in the [2025 final](/wiki/2025_CAF_Confederation_Cup_final "2025 CAF Confederation Cup final").

| [2025](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_CAF_Confederation_Cup "2024–25 CAF Confederation Cup") | [Morocco](/wiki/Royal_Moroccan_Football_Federation "Royal Moroccan Football Federation") [RS Berkane](/wiki/RS_Berkane "RS Berkane") (3) |

In 2004, CAF merged the [African Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/African_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "African Cup Winners' Cup") created in 1975 with the [CAF Cup](/wiki/CAF_Cup "CAF Cup") introduced in 1992 to form a new competition called the Confederation Cup, which has since become the secondary African club competition.

In the first edition, the Ghanaian club [Hearts of Oak](/wiki/Hearts_of_Oak_(football) "Hearts of Oak (football)") won the edition by beating another Ghanaian club, [Asante Kotoko](/wiki/Asante_Kotoko_S.C. "Asante Kotoko S.C.") in the final on [Penalties](/wiki/Penalty_kick_(association_football) "Penalty kick (association football)").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The following year, Moroccan club [AS FAR](/wiki/AS_FAR_(football_club) "AS FAR (football club)") won the cup against Nigeria's Dolphin FC.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In 2006, Tunisian club [Étoile du Sahel](/wiki/%C3%89toile_Sportive_du_Sahel "Étoile Sportive du Sahel") won the cup against Moroccan AS FAR (thanks to the away goals rule).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The Tunisian club [CS Sfaxien](/wiki/CS_Sfaxien "CS Sfaxien") won the cup in 2007 by beating the Sudanese Al Merreikh 5 goals to 2 in aggregate score (4–2, 1–0).[[5]](#cite_note-5) The following season, [Club Sfaxien](/wiki/Club_Sportif_Sfaxien "Club Sportif Sfaxien") again won the cup against another Tunisian club, [Étoile du Sahel](/wiki/%C3%89toile_Sportive_du_Sahel "Étoile Sportive du Sahel").[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 2009, [Stade Malien](/wiki/Stade_Malien "Stade Malien") won the edition by beating the Algerian club [ES Sétif](/wiki/ES_S%C3%A9tif "ES Sétif") in the final, on penalties.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The following season, the Moroccan club [Fath Union Sport](/wiki/Fath_Union_Sport "Fath Union Sport") won the cup against Tunisian [Club Sfaxien](/wiki/Club_Sportif_Sfaxien "Club Sportif Sfaxien"), winning the return match 3 to 2.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 2011, Moroccan club [Maghreb Fès](/wiki/Maghreb_de_F%C3%A8s "Maghreb de Fès") defeated Tunisia's [Club Africain](/wiki/Club_Africain "Club Africain") in the final, on penalties.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The following year, Congolese club [AC Léopards](/wiki/AC_L%C3%A9opards "AC Léopards") beat Malian club [Djoliba AC](/wiki/Djoliba_AC "Djoliba AC") in the final.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The 2013 edition saw CS Sfaxien win against Congolese [TP Mazembe](/wiki/TP_Mazembe "TP Mazembe").[[11]](#cite_note-11) In 2014, the Egyptian club [Al Ahly](/wiki/Al_Ahly_SC "Al Ahly SC") obtained its first confederation cup by beating the Ivorian club [Séwé FC](/wiki/S%C3%A9w%C3%A9_FC "Séwé FC").[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2015, [Étoile du Sahel](/wiki/%C3%89toile_Sportive_du_Sahel "Étoile Sportive du Sahel") again won the cup by beating South African club [Orlando Pirates](/wiki/Orlando_Pirates_F.C. "Orlando Pirates F.C.").[[13]](#cite_note-13) [TP Mazembe](/wiki/TP_Mazembe "TP Mazembe") achieved the double in 2016 and 2017, beating Algerian club [MO Béjaïa](/wiki/MO_B%C3%A9ja%C3%AFa "MO Béjaïa") and South African [SuperSport United](/wiki/SuperSport_United_F.C. "SuperSport United F.C.") respectively.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Moroccan club [Raja CA](/wiki/Raja_CA "Raja CA") won in 2018 against Congolese [AS Vita Club](/wiki/AS_Vita_Club "AS Vita Club").[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 2019, [Zamalek SC](/wiki/Zamalek_SC "Zamalek SC") beat Moroccan [RS Berkane](/wiki/RS_Berkane "RS Berkane") in the [final](/wiki/2019_CAF_Confederation_Cup_final "2019 CAF Confederation Cup final"), on [Penalties](/wiki/Penalty_kick_(association_football) "Penalty kick (association football)").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In [2020](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_CAF_Confederation_Cup "2020–21 CAF Confederation Cup") in the context of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the matches were then played behind closed doors, the Moroccan club [RS Berkane](/wiki/RS_Berkane "RS Berkane") beat the Egyptians of [Pyramids FC](/wiki/Pyramids_FC "Pyramids FC") by the score of 1 to 0.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Since this season, the final has been played in a single game. In [2021](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_CAF_Confederation_Cup "2021–22 CAF Confederation Cup"), the Moroccan club Raja CA won the cup for the second time by beating [JS Kabylie](/wiki/JS_Kabylie "JS Kabylie") in the [final](/wiki/2021_CAF_Confederation_Cup_Final "2021 CAF Confederation Cup Final") with a score of 2 to 1.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In [2022](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_CAF_Confederation_Cup "2022–23 CAF Confederation Cup"), Moroccan club RS Berkane won the cup for the second time, beating South African club [Orlando Pirates](/wiki/Orlando_Pirates "Orlando Pirates") in the final on penalties.[[20]](#cite_note-20) On 3 June 2023, [USM Alger](/wiki/USM_Alger "USM Alger") became the first [Algerian](/wiki/Algerian_Football_Federation "Algerian Football Federation") club to win the confederation cup after beating [Young Africans](/wiki/Young_Africans_S.C. "Young Africans S.C.") in the [2023 final](/wiki/2023_CAF_Confederation_Cup_final "2023 CAF Confederation Cup final").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In [2024](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_CAF_Confederation_Cup "2023–24 CAF Confederation Cup"), Egyptian Giant, [Zamalek SC](/wiki/Zamalek_SC "Zamalek SC") won the cup for the second time, beating Moroccan club [RS Berkane](/wiki/RS_Berkane "RS Berkane") in the final on away goals rule.[[22]](#cite_note-22) This was the second title for [Zamalek SC](/wiki/Zamalek_SC "Zamalek SC") after their win over the same team back in [2019](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_CAF_Confederation_Cup "2018–19 CAF Confederation Cup"), which made [Zamalek SC](/wiki/Zamalek_SC "Zamalek SC") the second most successful team after [CS Sfaxien](/wiki/CS_Sfaxien "CS Sfaxien") with 3 titles. On 25 May 2025, RS Berkane won its third title after defeating [Simba S.C.](/wiki/Simba_S.C. "Simba S.C.") in the final, becoming the joint-most successful club in the tournament's history.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23) This victory places the Moroccan side alongside Tunisia's CS Sfaxien, with both clubs now holding a record three titles each.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)

## Qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Confederation_Cup&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Qualification")]

The competition is composed of domestic cup winners from all 54 CAF member associations and the third-placed-finished club in the domestic leagues of the [top twelve-ranked associations](/wiki/CAF_5-year_ranking "CAF 5-year ranking") discounting/excluding the present year/season.

### Format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Confederation_Cup&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Format")]

The competition is played into two phases; the qualification phase and the main phase.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Qualification phase

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Confederation_Cup&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Qualification phase")]

The competition begins with a preliminary round and then a first qualifying round played in a "trim-down" knock-out format with the [away goals rule](/wiki/Away_goals_rule "Away goals rule") serving as tiebreakers.

### Main phase

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Confederation_Cup&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Main phase")]

* The sixteen winning teams from the second qualifying round enter the group stage divided into four groups of four. Each team will play against the other three opponents in a [round-robin](/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament") system [three points for a win](/wiki/Three_points_for_a_win "Three points for a win").
* The group winners and runners-up qualify to a [two-legged](/wiki/Two-legged_tie "Two-legged tie") knock-out rounds which shall be played in two matches, home and away in three rounds (quarter-finals, semi-finals and the finals).
* In case of equality in the number of goals scored during the two matches, the team scoring the greatest number of away goals will be declared winner. If the number of goals scored on the away matches is equal, kicks from the penalty mark will be taken.

### The Super Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Confederation_Cup&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: The Super Cup")]

The winners will face the [CAF Champions League](/wiki/CAF_Champions_League "CAF Champions League") winners in the [CAF Super Cup](/wiki/CAF_Super_Cup "CAF Super Cup") the following season on the former's home venue.

## Sponsorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Confederation_Cup&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Sponsorship")]

In October 2004, [MTN](/wiki/MTN_Group "MTN Group") contracted a four-year deal to sponsor CAF's competitions worth US$12.5 million, which at that time was the biggest sponsorship deal in African sporting history.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 2008, CAF put a value of [€](/wiki/Euro "Euro")100 million for a comprehensive and long-term package of its competitions when it opened tenders for a new sponsor, which was scooped up by French telecommunications giant [Orange](/wiki/Orange_S.A. "Orange S.A.") through the signing of an eight-year deal in July the following year, whose terms were not disclosed.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

On 21 July 2016, French energy and petroleum giant Total S.A. (renamed *[TotalEnergies](/wiki/TotalEnergies "TotalEnergies")* in 2021) secured an eight-year sponsorship package from CAF to [sponsor](/wiki/Sponsor_(commercial) "Sponsor (commercial)") its competitions, beginning with its flagship competition, the [Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Prizes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Confederation_Cup&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Prizes")]

### Trophy and medals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Confederation_Cup&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Trophy and medals")]

Each year, the winning team is presented with the African Champion Clubs' Cup, the current version of which has been awarded since the competition name change in 1997. Forty gold medals are presented to the competition winners and 40 silver medals to the runners-up. On May 25, 2025, CAF unveiled a new TotalEnergies CAF Confederation Cup trophy, marking a bold step in modernizing its competitions.[[37]](#cite_note-:1-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) The redesigned trophy symbolizes ambition, unity, and African pride, featuring a matte-gold football topped with a polished-gold map of Africa, supported by two upward-reaching arms to represent strength and solidarity.[[37]](#cite_note-:1-37) Its body combines shiny silver with a striking gold lightning streak to convey energy and competitiveness, while the marble base—engraved with the competition's name and past winners—adds a touch of tradition and prestige.[[37]](#cite_note-:1-37) Standing 45 cm tall and weighing between 8–10 kg, the new trophy reflects CAF's dedication to celebrating the excellence of African club football.[[37]](#cite_note-:1-37)
